question_preview: Double fertilisation is exhibited by
answer_span_text: Since two types of fusions, syngamy and triple fusion take place in an embryo sac the phenomenon is termed **double fertilisation**, an event unique to flowering plants.
all_quotes: Since two types of fusions, syngamy and triple fusion take place in an embryo sac the phenomenon is termed **double fertilisation**, an event unique to flowering plants.

quote_verifications.text: Since two types of fusions, syngamy and triple fusion take place in an embryo sac the phenomenon is termed **double fertilisation**, an event unique to flowering plants.

gpt_verification.gpt_reasoning: Double fertilisation, involving syngamy and triple fusion, is a characteristic feature unique to angiosperms.

notes: Hybrid match rank 1 (kw=0.8571, emb=1.0, combined=0.9429). Verified: high — The span explicitly states that double fertilisation is 'an event unique to flowering plants,' and flowering plants are angiosperms, which directly identifies option C and excludes algae, fungi, and gymnosperms.

levels_tried.fact: After entering one of the synergids, one of the male gametes moves towards the egg cell and fuses with its nucleus thus completing the syngamy. The other male gamete moves towards the two polar nuclei

fact_extraction.fact: After entering one of the synergids, one of the male gametes moves towards the egg cell and fuses with its nucleus thus completing the syngamy. The other male gamete moves towards the two polar nuclei located in the central cell and fuses with them to produce a triploid primary endosperm nucleus (PEN). As this involves the fusion of three haploid nuclei it is termed triple fusion. Since two types of fusions, syngamy and triple fusion take place in an embryo sac the phenomenon is termed double fertilisation, an event unique to angiosperms.

verification_result.reason: The span explicitly states that double fertilisation is 'an event unique to flowering plants,' and flowering plants are angiosperms, which directly identifies option C and excludes algae, fungi, and gymnosperms.

question_preview: The ovule of an angiosperm is technically equivalent to
section_title: 1.2.2 The Pistil, Megasporangium (ovule) and Embryo sac

gpt_verification.gpt_reasoning: In angiosperms, the ovule is an integumented megasporangium containing the nucellus and embryo sac, so it is technically equivalent to a megasporangium.

notes: Section-grounded (Tier 2) at rank 1 (kw=1.0, emb=1.0). Confidence: high. No verbatim quote — reasoning-based match.

levels_tried.fact: The ovule is a small structure attached to the placenta by means of a stalk called funicle. The body of the ovule fuses with funicle in the region called hilum. Thus, hilum represents the junction bet

levels_tried.span: The pistil has three parts – the stigma, style and the ovary.
levels_tried.verify_reason: The span only states the parts of the pistil and does not mention that an ovule is technically a megasporangium or provide any distinction from the other options.

levels_tried.span: The gymnosperms produce microspores and megaspores which are produced in microsporangia and megaspor
levels_tried.verify_reason: The span only states that megaspores are produced in megasporangia in gymnosperms and does not state the crucial fact that in angiosperms the ovule is technically the integumented megasporangium, so it cannot distinguish option A from the other options.

fact_extraction.fact: The ovule is a small structure attached to the placenta by means of a stalk called funicle. The body of the ovule fuses with funicle in the region called hilum. Thus, hilum represents the junction between ovule and funicle. Each ovule has one or two protective envelopes called integuments. Integuments encircle the ovule except at the tip where a small opening called the micropyle is organised. Opposite the micropylar end, is the chalaza, representing the basal part of the ovule. Enclosed within the integuments is a mass of cells called the nucellus. Cells of the nucellus have abundant reserve food materials. Located in the nucellus is the embryo sac or female gametophyte. An ovule generally has a single embryo sac formed from a megaspore through reduction division. One megaspore mother cell (MMC) differentiates in the micropylar region of the nucellus. It is a large cell containing dense cytoplasm and a prominent nucleus. The MMC undergoes meiotic division. Meiosis results in the production of four megaspores.

verification_result.reasoning: Yes. The paragraph explicitly states that megasporangia are commonly called ovules, and then again labels the section as “The Megasporangium (Ovule),” showing that an angiosperm ovule is technically the megasporangium. A student can therefore match ovule with megasporangium and choose option A.
key_facts_used: Arising from the placenta are the megasporangia, commonly called ovules.
The section heading states: “The Megasporangium (Ovule)”
The paragraph then describes the structure of a typical angiosperm ovule under the heading megasporangium.
verification_result.why_not_verbatim: The exact exam wording “technically equivalent to” is not used, but the paragraph directly equates ovule with megasporangium.

section_grounding.reasoning: Yes.
